   =20

Wouldn=E2=80=99t it be amazing to learn that GameStop Corp.=E2=80=99s whole=
 turnaround plan =E2=80=94=C2=A0the plan that turned it from an $18.84 stoc=
k at the end of 2020 to a $325 stock a month later, the plan that made it a=
 darling of retail investors, the plan that got it a bunch of  new high-pow=
ered executives to execute its move into online retail and also  ugh non-fu=
ngible tokens =E2=80=94=C2=A0was written by consultants at Boston Consultin=
g Group? Like if somewhere there was a PowerPoint deck from BCG, from 2020,=
 laying out how to become a meme stock? Before =E2=80=9Cmeme stock=E2=80=9D=
 was even a thing? Wouldn=E2=80=99t it be great if an internal BCG think ta=
nk invented meme stocks, and somehow last year=E2=80=99s wild Reddit enthus=
iasm for GameStop was all secretly created by consultants? That would be ve=
ry funny and also maybe the most impressive strategy-consulting success sto=
ry I=E2=80=99ve ever heard.But, no, that is not at all what happened. Inste=
ad=C2=A0BCG  did some other stuff for GameStop that=C2=A0didn=E2=80=99t=C2=
=A0work:Boston Consulting Group claims it hasn=E2=80=99t been paid about $3=
0 million in fees for its work =E2=80=9Csetting the company on a more susta=
inable path=E2=80=9D 2 1/2 years ago.The management consultant says that af=
ter it was hired in 2019 to bring GameStop out of a slump, a plan was agree=
d on to overhaul the business, including the video game and pre-owned elect=
ronics operations.Boston Consulting spent =E2=80=9Ctens of thousands of hou=
rs=E2=80=9D on the project and =E2=80=9Coverachieved=E2=80=9D by creating m=
ore profit improvement opportunities than were initially estimated, accordi=
ng to the complaint filed Tuesday in Delaware federal court.GameStop said i=
t will fight the suit, which it said lacks merit and reflects BCG=E2=80=99s=
 =E2=80=9Cprioritization of excessive fees over clients=E2=80=99 interests.=
=E2=80=9D=E2=80=9CIt is confounding that the high-priced consultants at BCG=
 claim to have delivered hundreds of millions in value for GameStop during =
a period when share price, sales and debt were at perilous levels,=E2=80=9D=
 GameStop said in an emailed statement. =E2=80=A6GameStop said it=E2=80=99s=
 =E2=80=9Cproud=E2=80=9D it no longer uses the consulting group=E2=80=99s s=
ervices.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CWe do not believe it is in our stockholders=E2=80=99=
 best interests to pay the tens of millions of dollars sought by BCG, espec=
ially given their seemingly meager impact on the company=E2=80=99s bottom l=
ine,=E2=80=9D GameStop said in the statement.Here is the complaint. The gis=
t of it is that GameStop hired BCG to make it more profitable, and agreed t=
o pay BCG a fixed fee for its work, plus additional success fees depending =
on how profitable BCG=E2=80=99s suggestions were. Or rather how profitable =
the suggestions were expected=C2=A0to be:BCG=E2=80=99s compensation was tie=
d directly to the anticipated profit improvements resulting from its work (=
i.e., the best possible estimate of each initiative=E2=80=99s expected impa=
ct at the time the decision to launch such initiative was made). In other w=
ords, BCG=E2=80=99s variable fees were based upon projections, not actual p=
rofit improvements. Indeed, the SOW provided that even 2019 profit improvem=
ents, and BCG=E2=80=99s resulting fee, were based upon projections.=C2=A0Th=
e concept of basing BCG=E2=80=99s variable fees on projected improvements r=
ather than actual results was negotiated and agreed upon by the parties spe=
cifically to ensure that BCG and GameStop=E2=80=99s incentives were aligned=
. This structure was intended to: incentivize BCG to significantly improve =
profits; prevent BCG from taking credit for and/or being penalized for exog=
enous factors outside the parties=E2=80=99 control; and to protect BCG from=
 additional factors, such as GameStop=E2=80=99s execution risk, i.e., GameS=
top failing to take the actions necessary to implement the plan and achieve=
 the predicted results.It lists the stuff that BCG was supposed to be worki=
ng on, and it is very consultant-y, things like =E2=80=9Cunlock both cost a=
nd operational improvements through a combination of organizational levers =
such as improved spans of control, a more effective reporting structure, an=
d redesigned roles and responsibilities=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cfacilitate Ga=
meStop=E2=80=99s ability to capture additional profit through revamped pric=
ing strategies, including revised pricing algorithms, markdown optimization=
, historical look at promotions, and other optimization levers.=E2=80=9D No=
t like =E2=80=9Cstart an NFT marketplace=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Chave the stoc=
k go to the moon and then do an at-the-market offering,=E2=80=9D or even re=
ally =E2=80=9Cpivot to online.=E2=80=9DAnyway, BCG would get a success fee =
if GameStop agreed that its suggestions=C2=A0would probably=C2=A0improve pr=
ofits, even if they did not actually improve profits.=C2=A0And then they ha=
d delightfully named mandatory meetings to talk about how much profit they =
were expecting:In order to calculate the anticipated profit improvements, a=
nd thus determine the amount of BCG=E2=80=99s variable fees, the SOW expres=
sly required GameStop to cooperate with BCG in its performance of the servi=
ces, including to provide timely access to data. The SOW also required that=
 the BCG team and GameStop leadership =E2=80=9Chold regular sessions: (i) t=
o address validation, approvals, testing and commencement and execution of =
the various initiatives so that all of the foregoing can be handled in a ti=
mely manner and (ii) to address financial target and planning coordination.=
=E2=80=9D These sessions were commonly referred to as =E2=80=9CThermometer =
Meetings.=E2=80=9D At all Thermometer Meetings, the SOW mandated the attend=
ance of GameStop=E2=80=99s Chief Transformation Officer and Chief Financial=
 Officer (or their designees), as well as the GameStop officer responsible =
for execution of the initiative being discussed. The purpose of these meeti=
ngs was to discuss and, as required by the SOW, agree upon financial baseli=
nes for each workstream and the anticipated profit improvement to each work=
stream, which would determine BCG=E2=80=99s fees. Thus, as explained above,=
 to minimize any disputes about factors outside of either party=E2=80=99s c=
ontrol, BCG=E2=80=99s fees were to be assessed based upon agreed-upon proje=
cted profits that GameStop was anticipated to achieve against agreed-upon b=
aselines, not actual profits realized.The lawsuit is not totally clear, but=
 as far as I can tell GameStop paid BCG its fixed fees but has not paid all=
 the money BCG thinks it is owed for improving GameStop=E2=80=99s profits. =
(Because the GameStop executives who hired BCG are long gone and the new ex=
ecutives have their own, different turnaround plans.) In fact GameStop=E2=
=80=99s profits do seem to have improved during the term of BCG=E2=80=99s e=
ngagement, by which I mean GameStop went from a net loss of $673 million in=
 2018 (before it hired BCG) to a net loss of $215 million in 2020 (after BC=
G had been working for a while),[1] which is a kind of profit improvement e=
ven if it is not an actual profit. But the complaint=E2=80=99s repeated emp=
hasis on projected profits makes me suspect that the actual results from th=
eir initiatives were not as good as the projections. (Again, perhaps becaus=
e the GameStop executives who hired BCG are long gone and GameStop went and=
 did other initiatives instead.)I can see why GameStop=E2=80=99s consultant=
s would want a $30 million success fee, since there is, you know, a lot of =
success floating around GameStop, but I can also see why GameStop wouldn=E2=
=80=99t want to pay them, since very little of that success seems to be due=
 to, you know, pricing strategy. They should send  RoaringKitty a $30 milli=
on success fee.

It looked for a while=C2=A0like Russia might default on its dollar-denomina=
ted government=C2=A0bonds that had an interest payment due last week. U.S. =
sanctions on Russia=E2=80=99s central bank and ministry of finance made it =
tricky for Russia=E2=80=99s correspondent bank and paying agent =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0the U.S. banks that handle its dollar transactions for it =E2=80=94 t=
o pass the dollar interest payments along to foreign investors. Sanctions a=
lso meant that=C2=A0Russia has limited access to its foreign reserves, so i=
t might not=C2=A0want to deplete those reserves by paying bondholders. It m=
ight also have wanted=C2=A0to stiff its U.S. and European bondholders, as a=
 sort of counter-sanction on Western economies. And Russia did make noises =
about maybe paying the interest in rubles. It seemed bad.I was too pessimis=
tic about all of this, and I figured that Russia would probably default. Bu=
t  it did not: It sent the dollars to its banks, the banks were able to sen=
d them along to the bondholders, and there has been no default;  everything=
 is fine, for now. Back when I was being pessimistic about the likelihood o=
f a default, I was=C2=A0very=C2=A0pessimistic about the=C2=A0consequences=
=C2=A0of a default.  I wrote:We are in a weird world of sovereign debt. His=
torically the reason countries defaulted on their debt was pretty much that=
 they ran out of money. When this happened, they would call up their credit=
ors and get in a room to negotiate some sort of restructuring. The creditor=
s would agree to give the country more time to pay them less money, and in =
exchange they=E2=80=99d eventually get something. There were many, many way=
s for this to go wrong, but in broad strokes it was basically a viable proc=
ess.=C2=A0But with Russia =E2=80=94 and also to some extent with Venezuela=
=E2=80=99s default in 2017 =E2=80=94 the reason for the default is geopolit=
ical, and there=E2=80=99s not really a way to get everyone in a room to res=
tructure. If U.S. sanctions forbid investors from doing financing transacti=
ons with a country, then it is very hard for those investors to negotiate a=
 restructuring. And if Russia is shut out of international financing market=
s anyway, there is not much incentive for it to negotiate a restructuring a=
nytime soon. =E2=80=A6So the likely outcome is that Russia will not pay int=
erest on those bonds, or will pay it in rubles. And then those bonds will b=
e in default. And Russia will say =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99re not really in defa=
ult; our default was caused by the mechanics of the international financial=
 system, not by our unwillingness or inability to pay.=E2=80=9D (It won=E2=
=80=99t be entirely wrong!) And Russia will not be all that interested in n=
egotiating a restructuring as long as it is at war and subject to sanctions=
, and even if it was interested its big international creditors won=E2=80=
=99t really be able to negotiate a restructuring anyway.=C2=A0Now, again, I=
 was wrong about Russia not paying interest on the bonds, so there was no d=
efault. But I still think the rest of it was right:=C2=A0If Russia had defa=
ulted last week (or if it does default in the near future), a restructuring=
 would be basically impossible. The problem is not that Russia is running o=
ut of money and wants its creditors to accept less. The problem is that the=
re are legal and geopolitical obstacles to Russia=C2=A0getting=C2=A0the mon=
ey to those creditors, and those exact same obstacles would prevent Russia =
and its creditors from sitting down in a room and working out an agreement.=
 Default would probably mean a weird limbo of nonpayment until Russia=E2=80=
=99s invasion of Ukraine, and the accompanying sanctions, end.Part of why t=
his has all worked out (so far) is that the U.S. has no real interest in st=
opping it.=C2=A0U.S. sanctions are designed to =E2=80=9Ceffectively immobil=
ize=C2=A0any assets=E2=80=9D of the Russian government, which seems like it=
 would prevent U.S. banks from passing along dollar=C2=A0bond payments, but=
 in fact the banks asked the U.S. authorities for permission to send the pa=
yments and  the authorities said yes. Presumably they=C2=A0figured that get=
ting Russian dollars to U.S. and European bondholders (1) was good for thos=
e bondholders and (2) wasn=E2=80=99t particularly helpful to Russia=E2=80=
=99s war effort.=C2=A0But the U.S. and the European Union=C2=A0do=C2=A0have=
 an interest in stopping other transactions =E2=80=94=C2=A0transactions in =
which, unlike bond payments, Russia continues to get something of value. Fo=
r instance  aircraft leases:Leasing firms doing business in Russia have dem=
anded the return of hundreds of Airbus SE and Boeing Co. planes to comply w=
ith economic sanctions imposed by the European Union and U.S. in response t=
o the invasion of Ukraine. Under EU rules, they have until March 28 to canc=
el contracts, but have no way of repossessing the aircraft after Russia mov=
ed to keep them within its borders.And so Russia,=C2=A0or rather Russian ai=
rlines,=C2=A0have essentially=C2=A0defaulted on those leases: The lessors h=
ave asked for the planes back, Russia has refused, and the lessors are not =
getting lease payments due to sanctions. And Russia=C2=A0does=C2=A0want to =
negotiate some sort of restructuring of these leases, so that (1) it can ke=
ep flying the planes and (2) it can retain enough goodwill in the capital m=
arkets to one day lease more planes again. But it can=E2=80=99t, for the re=
asons we talked about before: The lessors are=C2=A0not allowed to sit down =
to negotiate with Russia, because of the sanctions. Here=E2=80=99s  Christo=
pher Jasper at Bloomberg News:Authorities in Moscow are seeking ways to leg=
ally get round sanctions requiring international firms to recall the planes=
, Transport Minister Vitaly Savelyev said Tuesday. Options include payments=
 or an outright purchase of the jets, he said.Lessors have so far been unwi=
lling to negotiate on the matter, according to Savelyev. That=E2=80=99s mos=
t likely because any financial accord with Russian airlines would appear to=
 present a clear breach of the sanctions. =E2=80=A6Lessors stand to lose as=
 much as $10 billion as the value of their fleet in Russia declines. The pu=
rchase offer would ease the impact and give Russian airlines a potential pa=
thway for re-establishing business ties after the crisis passes. But moving=
 forward would put foreign lessors at risk of being penalized by authoritie=
s in the U.S., EU or other jurisdictions.The sanctions that caused the defa=
ult also prevent them from working it out.

Elsewhere in Russian  bond defaults caused by sanctions:=C2=A0A Russian com=
pany flush with cash is on the brink of missing a bond coupon payment after=
 Citigroup Inc. asked it to get an ironclad permit to pay from the U.S. Tre=
asury, according to two people familiar with the matter.Steelmaker=C2=A0Sev=
erstal=E2=80=99s $12.6 million coupon payment was originally=C2=A0due March=
 16. But paying and transfer agent Citigroup Inc. has required Severstal ge=
t permission from the U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control before it remit=
s the cash, the people said, declining to be identified because they aren=
=E2=80=99t authorized to speak publicly on the matter. A spokesperson for C=
itigroup declined to comment, as did Severstal=E2=80=99s press department.=
=C2=A0While the company itself is not sanctioned, controlling shareholder=
=C2=A0Alexey Mordashov=C2=A0is on the European Union and U.K.=E2=80=99s san=
ctions lists. Still, because he=E2=80=99s not included in any U.S. sanction=
s, Severstal refrained from preemptively applying for an OFAC permit, one o=
f the people said. A spokesperson for the U.S. Treasury couldn=E2=80=99t co=
mment on dealings with a company or individual the nation hasn=E2=80=99t sa=
nctioned. =E2=80=A6Severstal said in a March 16 press release that it had r=
eceived permission from the Russian government to make the interest payment=
 in dollars, but that it had grounds to believe that Citigroup would block =
the payment. Instead of transferring the full amount owed, Severstal decide=
d to make a test payment, which as of the end of last week had not been pro=
cessed, people familiar with the matter said at the time.Weird! Russia=E2=
=80=99s government definitely=C2=A0is=C2=A0sanctioned, and Citigroup is the=
 paying agent on its dollar bonds. It processed a bond payment for Russia l=
ast week, presumably after checking in with OFAC and getting, perhaps not a=
 permit, but probably pretty ironclad assurances that no one at Citi would =
go to jail for passing along the payment. Not sure why Severstal, which is =
not sanctioned, is having a harder time.

 European gas prices rose as much as 21% in Amsterdam amid concerns that Ru=

 that has happened: Europe buys Russian gas, it pays Russia in euros, and t=

.Some companies kept in touch with VTB, Russia=E2=80=99s second-biggest ban=

n said, asking not to be identified discussing private matters.=E2=80=9CI b=
ought all these nice Russian stocks for you, when do you want to pick them =
up,=E2=80=9D VTB emails its clients, but they do not reply.Also the Russian=

 invasion of Ukraine.The Moscow Exchange will resume trading in 33 Russian =
equities, including some of the biggest companies such as Gazprom PJSC and =

pply, it said, adding to an earlier restriction on foreigners exiting local=

ongest closure in the country=E2=80=99s modern history.=C2=A0And at the Rus=
sian central bank, people are quitting so fast that there  aren=E2=80=99t e=

 hands to terminate accounts. Arrows plastered along passageways steered em=

      SEC ESG
   =20
 =20
One thing that people like to say is that it is expensive to be a U.S.=C2=
=A0public company, and it keeps getting more expensive. If you are a privat=
e company, you can write your financial statements on a napkin and text a p=
icture of them to your investors, and if the investors are fine with that t=
hen that=E2=80=99s fine. If you are a public company, you need to get the f=
inancial statements audited and file them with the U.S. Securities and Exch=
ange Commission, and the SEC keeps adding new rules about what you need to =
disclose:=C2=A0about executive pay, about cybersecurity risk, about conflic=
t diamonds, etc.=C2=A0All of these things cost money, as you have to hire s=
pecialized lawyers and accountants to track them and write the disclosure.=
=C2=A0As it gets more expensive to be public, public companies are bigger a=
nd older than they used to be: If you are a smallish private company, you c=
an=E2=80=99t afford to go public. This often means that public companies ar=
e=C2=A0slower-growing=C2=A0than they used to be: It used to be that smaller=
 companies could go public before they were huge and profitable, and public=
 investors could invest in them early while they were still growing. Amazon=
.com went public at a $438 million valuation=C2=A0in 1997; that year there =
were 174 tech IPOs with a mean and median market capitalization of $264 mil=
lion and $113 million, respectively, according to Jay Ritter=E2=80=99s data=
.=C2=A0 In 2021 those numbers were 118 tech IPOs with mean and median marke=
t caps of $6.3 billion and $3 billion. Fewer IPOs, and for bigger companies=
 with less growth ahead of them.I have  argued in the past that this change=
 is not=C2=A0just=C2=A0about it being more expensive to go public: There is=
 a lot more private capital available, and there are more technologies for =
that capital to find private companies, so it is easier to grow big while s=
taying private than it used to be. But, sure, at some margin it is also har=
der to go public than it used to be.=C2=A0People often think this is bad. G=
enerally speaking, ordinary investors can buy stock in public companies, bu=
t  not in private ones. If the fast-growing companies are all private, then=
 ordinary investors will miss out on a lot of growth.=C2=A0We  talked yeste=
rday about the SEC=E2=80=99s proposed rules for climate-related disclosure.=
 One thing that I did not mention, but that is worth emphasizing, is that t=
hese rules will be expensive. Companies will need to calculate and disclose=
 their emissions, and hire attestation firms to audit those disclosures, an=
d write extensive disclosures of their plans for responding to climate chan=
ge. All of this will create a lot of jobs for lawyers and emissions atteste=
rs and environmental planners, and people with those jobs will get their pa=
ychecks from public companies.And at the margin some companies =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0some medium-sized, medium-growth, maturing private companies =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0will decide not to go public, because it=E2=80=99s too expensive. Eac=
h addition to the disclosure regime costs a bit of money and deters going p=
ublic a little bit, but the climate rules are not so much an addition to th=
e disclosure regime as they are a whole new disclosure regime. That will co=
st a lot of money and deter going public more. Here is a client memo from D=
avis, Polk & Wardwell LLP:The proposed rules are highly complex, and compli=
ance costs are likely to be significant, which may impact the willingness o=
f private companies to go public.=C2=A0...The proposal also does not includ=
e any incremental phase-in allowance for newly public companies, which coul=
d have the impact of discouraging or delaying plans of private companies to=
 go public in the United States. In particular, foreign companies (who have=
 in recent years flocked to the United States to raise capital) may be dete=
rred from doing so in view of the burdens associated with the proposal.Inte=
rnal costs associated with the proposed rule will likely be significant due=
 to the need to build in disclosure controls and create board, management a=
nd risk processes and procedures. Moreover, tracking and verifying the requ=
ired data for Scope 1 and Scope 2 disclosures will require significant inve=
stments in talent and technology. In addition, professional expenses associ=
ated with required disclosures will likely be significant due to the third-=
party attestation report requirement, as well as the inclusion of climate-r=
elated disclosure in financial statements subject to audit review.But this =
is not purely a function of the SEC=E2=80=99s proposed rules. Public market=
s were already heading in this direction. Public-market investors are incre=
asingly likely to have environmental, social and governance mandates, so th=
ey were already pushing companies to disclose more about ESG and achieve cl=
imate goals. These demands from investors are softer and more nuanced than =
the SEC=E2=80=99s rules =E2=80=94=C2=A0investors tend to be more interested=
 in, you know, Exxon Mobil Corp.=E2=80=99s plans to reduce emissions than t=
hey are in the greenhouse gas accounting of a tech startup that just went p=
ublic =E2=80=94 but if you were considering an IPO last week, before the SE=
C=E2=80=99s proposal, your list of considerations would probably include so=
mething like =E2=80=9Cif I go public I=E2=80=99m going to hear a lot more f=
rom shareholders about ESG stuff than if I stay private.=E2=80=9D The SEC=
=E2=80=99s rules will add a lot to the costs and complexity of that conside=
ration, but broadly speaking the SEC is kind of aligned with investor deman=
d here.I suspect part of the result will be a change in the=C2=A0types=C2=
=A0of companies that go, or remain, public. If you are a green technology c=
ompany, doing all the climate reporting will be relatively easy for you, an=
d you will be rewarded for it; investors want to read about how good your c=
limate transition plan is. The SEC=E2=80=99s 510-page rules proposal is mos=
tly about quantifying and disclosing climate risk, but a company =E2=80=9Cm=
ay also disclose, as applicable, the actual and potential impacts of any cl=
imate-related opportunities it is pursuing.=E2=80=9D If you have a good cli=
mate story, the SEC will let you tell it.Meanwhile if you are a fracking co=
mpany, ehhh, you are not going public. If you are a fracker that is=C2=A0al=
ready=C2=A0public, you are thinking about going private. The SEC rules, and=
 investor demand for ESG investments, will add a lot to your expenses, and =
in an unpleasant way: You=E2=80=99re going to pay a ton of money for a repo=
rt saying that you emit too much carbon and that your business might not be=
 viable in a climate transition. (You=E2=80=99re going to have to hire a cl=
imate-disclosure specialist, and presumably climate-disclosure specialists =
will charge a premium to work for frackers.)=C2=A0The basic result is =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0well, we have  talked about it before. The result is that U.S. =
public markets will be increasingly ESG-y, due to both SEC regulation and i=
nvestor demand, and companies that do not appeal to ESG investors will go o=
r stay private. Green companies will be public, to capitalize on demand fro=
m ESG-ish public investors, while dirty companies will be private, to capit=
alize on, you know, not having to write reports about how dirty they are. T=
he companies whose climate risks are most worrying are the ones who won=E2=
=80=99t disclose them.That said! I wrote yesterday that the SEC=E2=80=99s p=
roposed =E2=80=9Cdisclosure regime effectively deputizes public companies t=
o be climate enforcers.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Many big public companies will have t=
o disclose their Scope 3 emissions, meaning all the emissions in their valu=
e chain, including by customers and suppliers. If a public company=E2=80=99=
s customers and suppliers are private companies, the SEC kind of expects th=
e public company to work=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cwith its suppliers and downstream di=
stributors to take steps to reduce those entities=E2=80=99 Scopes 1 and 2 e=
missions,=E2=80=9D so that it can report its Scope 3 emissions. Public comp=
anies are everywhere, and even companies that don=E2=80=99t go public thems=
elves might still have to care about these rules.

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Wouldn=E2=80=99t it be amazing to lear=

an that turned it from an $18.84 stock at the end of 2020 to a $325 stock a=

 that got it a bunch of <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/=

ine retail and also <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/clic=

rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> ugh non-fungible tokens=
</a> =E2=80=94=C2=A0was written by consultants at Boston Consulting Group? =
Like if somewhere there was a PowerPoint deck from BCG, from 2020, laying o=
ut how to become a meme stock? Before =E2=80=9Cmeme stock=E2=80=9D was even=
 a thing? Wouldn=E2=80=99t it be great if an internal BCG think tank invent=
ed meme stocks, and somehow last year=E2=80=99s wild Reddit enthusiasm for =
GameStop was all secretly created by consultants? That would be very funny =
and also maybe the most impressive strategy-consulting success story I=E2=
=80=99ve ever heard.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But, no, that is not a=
t all what happened. Instead=C2=A0BCG <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomber=
gbusiness.com/click/27125022.276601/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXd=
zL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMjMvZ2FtZXN0b3Atc3VlZC1ieS1iY2ctb3Zlci0zMC1taWxsaW=
9uLWJpbGwtdG8tb3ZlcmhhdWwtY29tcGFueT9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzIzMjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1d=
G1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjAzMjMmdXRt=
X2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB06e221b9" itemprop=3D"=
StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #00000=
0; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-botto=
m-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> did=
 some other stuff for GameStop that=C2=A0<em>didn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0</em>work<=
/a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Boston Consulting Group cl=
aims it hasn=E2=80=99t been paid about $30 million in fees for its work =E2=
=80=9Csetting the company on a more sustainable path=E2=80=9D 2 1/2 years a=
go.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The management consultant says that aft=
er it was hired in 2019 to bring GameStop out of a slump, a plan was agreed=
 on to overhaul the business, including the video game and pre-owned electr=
onics operations.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Boston Consulting spent =
=E2=80=9Ctens of thousands of hours=E2=80=9D on the project and =E2=80=9Cov=
erachieved=E2=80=9D by creating more profit improvement opportunities than =
were initially estimated, according to the complaint filed Tuesday in Delaw=
are federal court.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">GameStop said it will fi=
ght the suit, which it said lacks merit and reflects BCG=E2=80=99s =E2=80=
=9Cprioritization of excessive fees over clients=E2=80=99 interests.=E2=80=
=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CIt is confounding that the hig=
h-priced consultants at BCG claim to have delivered hundreds of millions in=
 value for GameStop during a period when share price, sales and debt were a=
t perilous levels,=E2=80=9D GameStop said in an emailed statement. =E2=80=
=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">GameStop said it=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cpro=
ud=E2=80=9D it no longer uses the consulting group=E2=80=99s services.=C2=
=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CWe do not believe it is in our=
 stockholders=E2=80=99 best interests to pay the tens of millions of dollar=
s sought by BCG, especially given their seemingly meager impact on the comp=
any=E2=80=99s bottom line,=E2=80=9D GameStop said in the statement.</p></bl=
ockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.=
bloombergbusiness.com/click/27125022.276601/aHR0cHM6Ly9hc3NldHMuYndieC5pby9=
kb2N1bWVudHMvdXNlcnMvaXFqV0hCRmRmeElVL3JmeVJFMFBQeThCdy92MA/607f07ceef4b852=
4a319a23cBbe5400e1" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;">the complaint</a>. The gist of it is that Gam=
eStop hired BCG to make it more profitable, and agreed to pay BCG a fixed f=
ee for its work, plus additional success fees depending on how profitable B=
CG=E2=80=99s suggestions were. Or rather how profitable the suggestions wer=
e <em>expected=C2=A0</em>to be:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"=
>BCG=E2=80=99s compensation was tied directly to the anticipated profit imp=
rovements resulting from its work (i.e., the best possible estimate of each=
 initiative=E2=80=99s expected impact at the time the decision to launch su=
ch initiative was made). In other words, BCG=E2=80=99s variable fees were b=
ased upon projections, <strong>not</strong> actual profit improvements. Ind=
eed, the SOW provided that even 2019 profit improvements, and BCG=E2=80=99s=
 resulting fee, were based upon projections.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 1=
6px 0;">The concept of basing BCG=E2=80=99s variable fees on projected impr=
ovements rather than actual results was negotiated and agreed upon by the p=
arties specifically to ensure that BCG and GameStop=E2=80=99s incentives we=
re aligned. This structure was intended to: incentivize BCG to significantl=
y improve profits; prevent BCG from taking credit for and/or being penalize=
d for exogenous factors outside the parties=E2=80=99 control; and to protec=
t BCG from additional factors, such as GameStop=E2=80=99s execution risk, i=
.e., GameStop failing to take the actions necessary to implement the plan a=
nd achieve the predicted results.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px =
0;">It lists the stuff that BCG was supposed to be working on, and it is ve=
ry consultant-y, things like =E2=80=9Cunlock both cost and operational impr=
ovements through a combination of organizational levers such as improved sp=
ans of control, a more effective reporting structure, and redesigned roles =
and responsibilities=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cfacilitate GameStop=E2=80=99s ab=
ility to capture additional profit through revamped pricing strategies, inc=
luding revised pricing algorithms, markdown optimization, historical look a=
t promotions, and other optimization levers.=E2=80=9D Not like =E2=80=9Csta=
rt an NFT marketplace=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Chave the stock go to the moon an=
d then do an at-the-market offering,=E2=80=9D or even really =E2=80=9Cpivot=
 to online.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway, BCG would get =
a success fee if GameStop agreed that its suggestions=C2=A0<em>would probab=
ly=C2=A0</em>improve profits, even if they did not actually improve profits=
.=C2=A0And then they had delightfully named mandatory meetings to talk abou=
t how much profit they were expecting:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 1=
6px 0;">In order to calculate the anticipated profit improvements, and thus=
 determine the amount of BCG=E2=80=99s variable fees, the SOW expressly req=
uired GameStop to cooperate with BCG in its performance of the services, in=
cluding to provide timely access to data. The SOW also required that the BC=
G team and GameStop leadership =E2=80=9Chold regular sessions: (i) to addre=
ss validation, approvals, testing and commencement and execution of the var=
ious initiatives so that all of the foregoing can be handled in a timely ma=
nner and (ii) to address financial target and planning coordination.=E2=80=
=9D These sessions were commonly referred to as =E2=80=9CThermometer Meetin=
gs.=E2=80=9D At all Thermometer Meetings, the SOW mandated the attendance o=
f GameStop=E2=80=99s Chief Transformation Officer and Chief Financial Offic=
er (or their designees), as well as the GameStop officer responsible for ex=
ecution of the initiative being discussed. The purpose of these meetings wa=
s to discuss and, as required by the SOW, agree upon financial baselines fo=
r each workstream and the <em><strong>anticipated </strong></em>profit impr=
ovement to each workstream, which would determine BCG=E2=80=99s fees. Thus,=
 as explained above, to minimize any disputes about factors outside of eith=
er party=E2=80=99s control, BCG=E2=80=99s fees were to be assessed based up=
on agreed-upon <em><strong>projected </strong></em>profits that GameStop wa=
s anticipated to achieve against agreed-upon baselines, not actual profits =
realized.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The lawsuit is not t=
otally clear, but as far as I can tell GameStop paid BCG its fixed fees but=
 has not paid all the money BCG thinks it is owed for improving GameStop=E2=
=80=99s profits. (Because the GameStop executives who hired BCG are long go=
ne and the new executives have their own, different turnaround plans.) In f=
act GameStop=E2=80=99s profits do seem to have improved during the term of =
BCG=E2=80=99s engagement, by which I mean GameStop <a href=3D"https://link.=
mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27125022.276601/aHR0cHM6Ly9nYW1lc3RvcC5nY3=
Mtd2ViLmNvbS9ub2RlLzE4NjYxL2h0bWwjaTNhZDY1Yzg1ODRhNDQ1ZWU5NGU0MzE0ZjY3Y2U2M=
TZjXzEyNw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB0e68bcfb" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemsco=
pe=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration=
: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; =
border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">went from</a> a net lo=
ss of $673 million in 2018 (before it hired BCG) to a net loss of $215 mill=
ion in 2020 (after BCG had been working for a while),[1] which is a kind of=
 profit improvement even if it is not an actual profit. But the complaint=
=E2=80=99s repeated emphasis on projected profits makes me suspect that the=
 actual results from their initiatives were not as good as the projections.=
 (Again, perhaps because the GameStop executives who hired BCG are long gon=
e and GameStop went and did other initiatives instead.)</p><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">I can see why GameStop=E2=80=99s consultants would want a $30 =
million success fee, since there is, you know, a lot of success floating ar=
ound GameStop, but I can also see why GameStop wouldn=E2=80=99t want to pay=
 them, since very little of that success seems to be due to, you know, pric=
ing strategy. They should send <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusine=
ss.com/click/27125022.276601/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2=
FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDktMTcvYmUtY2FyZWZ1bC13aXRoLXlvdXItZmluYW5jaWFsLWluZmx1Z=
W5jZT9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzIzMjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3Vy=
Y2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjAzMjMmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607=
f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB677130a9" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsco=
pe" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impor=

</td>

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It looked for a while=C2=A0like Russia=
 might default on its dollar-denominated government=C2=A0bonds that had an =
interest payment due last week. U.S. sanctions on Russia=E2=80=99s central =
bank and ministry of finance made it tricky for Russia=E2=80=99s correspond=
ent bank and paying agent =E2=80=94=C2=A0the U.S. banks that handle its dol=
lar transactions for it =E2=80=94 to pass the dollar interest payments alon=
g to foreign investors. Sanctions also meant that=C2=A0Russia has limited a=
ccess to its foreign reserves, so it might not=C2=A0want to deplete those r=
eserves by paying bondholders. It might also have <em>wanted=C2=A0</em>to s=
tiff its U.S. and European bondholders, as a sort of counter-sanction on We=
stern economies. And Russia did make noises about maybe paying the interest=
 in rubles. It seemed bad.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I was too pessim=
istic about all of this, and I figured that Russia would probably default. =
But <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27125022.27660=
1/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMTcvanBtb=

 solid; background-color: #ccc;"> it did not</a>: It sent the dollars to it=
s banks, the banks were able to send them along to the bondholders, and the=
re has been no default; <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/=

 for now. Back when I was being pessimistic about the likelihood of a defau=
lt, I was=C2=A0<em>very=C2=A0</em>pessimistic about the=C2=A0<em>consequenc=
es=C2=A0</em>of a default. <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.c=
om/click/27125022.276601/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydG=

1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;"> I wrote</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We a=
re in a weird world of sovereign debt. Historically the reason countries de=
faulted on their debt was pretty much that they ran out of money. When this=
 happened, they would call up their creditors and get in a room to negotiat=
e some sort of restructuring. The creditors would agree to give the country=
 more time to pay them less money, and in exchange they=E2=80=99d eventuall=
y get something. There were many, many ways for this to go wrong, but in br=
oad strokes it was basically a viable process.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">But with Russia =E2=80=94 and also to some extent with Venezuela=
=E2=80=99s default in 2017 =E2=80=94 the reason for the default is geopolit=
ical, and there=E2=80=99s not really a way to get everyone in a room to res=
tructure. If U.S. sanctions forbid investors from doing financing transacti=
ons with a country, then it is very hard for those investors to negotiate a=
 restructuring. And if Russia is shut out of international financing market=
s anyway, there is not much incentive for it to negotiate a restructuring a=
nytime soon. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So the likely outcom=
e is that Russia will not pay interest on those bonds, or will pay it in ru=
bles. And then those bonds will be in default. And Russia will say =E2=80=
=9Cwe=E2=80=99re not really in default; our default was caused by the mecha=
nics of the international financial system, not by our unwillingness or ina=
bility to pay.=E2=80=9D (It won=E2=80=99t be entirely wrong!) And Russia wi=
ll not be all that interested in negotiating a restructuring as long as it =
is at war and subject to sanctions, and even if it was interested its big i=
nternational creditors won=E2=80=99t really be able to negotiate a restruct=
uring anyway.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Now, again=
, I was wrong about Russia not paying interest on the bonds, so there was n=
o default. But I still think the rest of it was right:=C2=A0If Russia had d=
efaulted last week (or if it does default in the near future), a restructur=
ing would be basically impossible. The problem is not that Russia is runnin=
g out of money and wants its creditors to accept less. The problem is that =
there are legal and geopolitical obstacles to Russia=C2=A0<em>getting=C2=A0=
</em>the money to those creditors, and those exact same obstacles would pre=
vent Russia and its creditors from sitting down in a room and working out a=
n agreement. Default would probably mean a weird limbo of nonpayment until =
Russia=E2=80=99s invasion of Ukraine, and the accompanying sanctions, end.<=
/p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Part of why this has all worked out (so far=
) is that the U.S. has no real interest in stopping it.=C2=A0<a href=3D"htt=
ps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27125022.276601/aHR0cHM6Ly9ob21l=

ank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; ba=
ckground-color: #ccc;">U.S. sanctions</a> are designed to =E2=80=9Ceffectiv=
ely immobilize=C2=A0any assets=E2=80=9D of the Russian government, which se=
ems like it would prevent U.S. banks from passing along dollar=C2=A0bond pa=
yments, but in fact the banks asked the U.S. authorities for permission to =
send the payments and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/cl=

at getting Russian dollars to U.S. and European bondholders (1) was good fo=

px 0;">Leasing firms doing business in Russia have demanded the return of h=

n its borders.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so Russia,=

 And Russia=C2=A0<em>does=C2=A0</em>want to negotiate some sort of restruct=

he lessors are=C2=A0<em>not allowed to sit down to negotiate with Russia</e=

gin: 16px 0;">Authorities in Moscow are seeking ways to legally get round s=
anctions requiring international firms to recall the planes, Transport Mini=
ster Vitaly Savelyev said Tuesday. Options include payments or an outright =
purchase of the jets, he said.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Lessors have=
 so far been unwilling to negotiate on the matter, according to Savelyev. T=
hat=E2=80=99s most likely because any financial accord with Russian airline=
s would appear to present a clear breach of the sanctions. =E2=80=A6</p><p =
style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Lessors stand to lose as much as $10 billion as t=
he value of their fleet in Russia declines. The purchase offer would ease t=
he impact and give Russian airlines a potential pathway for re-establishing=
 business ties after the crisis passes. But moving forward would put foreig=
n lessors at risk of being penalized by authorities in the U.S., EU or othe=
r jurisdictions.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The sanctions=
 that caused the default also prevent them from working it out.</p><table c=

te><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A Russian company flush with cash is on the=
 brink of missing a bond coupon payment after Citigroup Inc. asked it to ge=
t an ironclad permit to pay from the U.S. Treasury, according to two people=
 familiar with the matter.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Steelmaker=C2=A0=
Severstal=E2=80=99s $12.6 million coupon payment was originally=C2=A0due Ma=
rch 16. But paying and transfer agent Citigroup Inc. has required Severstal=
 get permission from the U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control before it re=
mits the cash, the people said, declining to be identified because they are=
n=E2=80=99t authorized to speak publicly on the matter. A spokesperson for =
Citigroup declined to comment, as did Severstal=E2=80=99s press department.=
=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">While the company itself is not sanc=
tioned, controlling shareholder=C2=A0Alexey Mordashov=C2=A0is on the Europe=
an Union and U.K.=E2=80=99s sanctions lists. Still, because he=E2=80=99s no=
t included in any U.S. sanctions, Severstal refrained from preemptively app=
lying for an OFAC permit, one of the people said. A spokesperson for the U.=
S. Treasury couldn=E2=80=99t comment on dealings with a company or individu=
al the nation hasn=E2=80=99t sanctioned. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 1=
6px 0;">Severstal said in a March 16 press release that it had received per=
mission from the Russian government to make the interest payment in dollars=
, but that it had grounds to believe that Citigroup would block the payment=
. Instead of transferring the full amount owed, Severstal decided to make a=
 test payment, which as of the end of last week had not been processed, peo=
ple familiar with the matter said at the time.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"=

 discouraging or delaying plans of private companies to go public in the Un=
ited States. In particular, foreign companies (who have in recent years flo=
cked to the United States to raise capital) may be deterred from doing so i=
n view of the burdens associated with the proposal.</p><p style=3D"margin: =
16px 0;">Internal costs associated with the proposed rule will likely be si=
gnificant due to the need to build in disclosure controls and create board,=
 management and risk processes and procedures. Moreover, tracking and verif=
ying the required data for Scope 1 and Scope 2 disclosures will require sig=
nificant investments in talent and technology. In addition, professional ex=
penses associated with required disclosures will likely be significant due =
to the third-party attestation report requirement, as well as the inclusion=
 of climate-related disclosure in financial statements subject to audit rev=
iew.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But this is not purely a =
function of the SEC=E2=80=99s proposed rules. Public markets were already h=
eading in this direction. Public-market investors are increasingly likely t=
o have environmental, social and governance mandates, so they were already =
pushing companies to disclose more about ESG and achieve climate goals. The=
se demands from investors are softer and more nuanced than the SEC=E2=80=99=
s rules =E2=80=94=C2=A0investors tend to be more interested in, you know, E=
xxon Mobil Corp.=E2=80=99s plans to reduce emissions than they are in the g=
reenhouse gas accounting of a tech startup that just went public =E2=80=94 =
but if you were considering an IPO last week, before the SEC=E2=80=99s prop=
osal, your list of considerations would probably include something like =E2=
=80=9Cif I go public I=E2=80=99m going to hear a lot more from shareholders=
 about ESG stuff than if I stay private.=E2=80=9D The SEC=E2=80=99s rules w=
ill add a lot to the costs and complexity of that consideration, but broadl=
y speaking the SEC is kind of aligned with investor demand here.</p><p styl=
e=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I suspect part of the result will be a change in the=
=C2=A0<em>types=C2=A0</em>of companies that go, or remain, public. If you a=
re a green technology company, doing all the climate reporting will be rela=
tively easy for you, and you will be rewarded for it; investors want to rea=
d about how good your climate transition plan is. The SEC=E2=80=99s <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27125022.276601/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9ydWxlcy9wcm9wb3NlZC8yMDIyLzMzLTExMDQyLnBkZg/607f07ceef4b8=
524a319a23cB114be31e" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;">510-page rules proposal</a> is mostly about q=
uantifying and disclosing climate <em>risk</em>, but a company =E2=80=9Cmay=
 also disclose, as applicable, the actual and potential impacts of any clim=
ate-related opportunities it is pursuing.=E2=80=9D If you have a good clima=
te story, the SEC will let you tell it.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Mea=
nwhile if you are a fracking company, ehhh, you are not going public. If yo=
u are a fracker that is=C2=A0<em>already=C2=A0</em>public, you are thinking=
 about going private. The SEC rules, and investor demand for ESG investment=
s, will add a lot to your expenses, and in an unpleasant way: You=E2=80=99r=
e going to pay a ton of money for a report saying that you emit too much ca=
rbon and that your business might not be viable in a climate transition. (Y=
ou=E2=80=99re going to have to hire a climate-disclosure specialist, and pr=
esumably climate-disclosure specialists will charge a premium to work for f=
rackers.)=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The basic result is =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0well, we have <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/c=
lick/27125022.276601/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbG=
VzLzIwMjEtMDctMDgvc29tZW9uZS1pcy1nb2luZy10by1kcmlsbC10aGUtb2lsP2NtcGlkPUJCR=
DAzMjMyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVy=
JnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMDMyMyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319=
a23cB42e2569d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_bl=
ank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; ba=
ckground-color: #ccc;"> talked about it before</a>. The result is that U.S.=
 public markets will be increasingly ESG-y, due to both SEC regulation and =
investor demand, and companies that do not appeal to ESG investors will go =
or stay private. Green companies will be public, to capitalize on demand fr=
om ESG-ish public investors, while dirty companies will be private, to capi=
talize on, you know, not having to write reports about how dirty they are. =
The companies whose climate risks are most worrying are the ones who won=E2=
=80=99t disclose them.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That said! I wrote y=
esterday that the SEC=E2=80=99s proposed =E2=80=9Cdisclosure regime effecti=
vely deputizes public companies to be climate enforcers.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Many=
 big public companies will have to disclose their Scope 3 emissions, meanin=
g all the emissions in their value chain, including by customers and suppli=
ers. If a public company=E2=80=99s customers and suppliers are private comp=
anies, the SEC kind of expects the public company to work=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cwit=
h its suppliers and downstream distributors to take steps to reduce those e=
ntities=E2=80=99 Scopes 1 and 2 emissions,=E2=80=9D so that it can report i=
ts Scope 3 emissions. Public companies are everywhere, and even companies t=
hat don=E2=80=99t go public themselves might still have to care about these=
 rules.</p><table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=
=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0">
